cot: The premises state that if a Python project is well-tested, then it is optimized.
In logic, this is a conditional statement of the form "If P, then Q", where P is "well-tested" and Q is "optimized".
The contrapositive of a conditional statement "If P, then Q" is logically equivalent and takes the form "If not Q, then not P".
Applying the contrapositive rule to the given premise, we derive that if a Python project is not optimized, then it is not well-tested.
Therefore, the premises logically entail the conclusion that if a Python project is not optimized, then it is not well-tested.
From Premise 10, we know that every Python project is well-structured.
Premise 7 states that if a Python project is well-structured, then it is optimized.
Since all Python projects are well-structured (Premise 10), we can apply the rule in Premise 7 to every project.
Therefore, every Python project must be optimized.
This means that the statement "If all Python projects are well-structured, then all Python projects are optimized" logically follows from the premises.

John has completed all required courses.
Since John has completed all required courses, he is eligible for graduation.
John maintains a GPA of 3.8, which is above 3.5.
Combined with his eligibility for graduation, John's GPA of 3.8 means he graduates with honors.
John has completed a thesis.
Since John graduates with honors and completes a thesis, he receives academic distinction.
If John receives academic distinction, he qualifies for the graduate fellowship program.
Therefore, John qualifies for the graduate fellowship program.
John has completed all required courses, as stated in Premise 5.
John maintains a GPA of 3.8, which is above the 3.5 threshold, as mentioned in Premise 6.
Since John has completed all required courses and maintains a GPA above 3.5, he is eligible for graduation, according to Premise 1 and Premise 2.
John has also completed a thesis, as stated in Premise 7.
If a student graduates with honors and completes a thesis, they receive academic distinction, as outlined in Premise 3.
John graduates with honors (as inferred in Step 3) and has completed a thesis (as stated in Step 4), so it follows that John receives academic distinction.
Since Professor John has completed pedagogical training, he can teach undergraduate courses according to Premise 1.
Combined with Premise 1 and Premise 6, Professor John can supervise graduate students because he can teach undergraduate courses and holds a PhD.
From Premise 2 and Premise 7, Professor John can serve on curriculum committees because he can supervise graduate students and has at least 3 publications.
Using Premise 3 and Premise 8, Professor John can propose new courses because he can serve on curriculum committees and has a positive teaching evaluation.
Therefore, the conclusion that Professor John can propose new courses follows logically from the premises.
Since Professor John has completed pedagogical training, he satisfies the requirement to teach undergraduate courses.
Combined with holding a PhD, Professor John is qualified to supervise graduate students.
With at least 3 publications, Professor John qualifies for serving on curriculum committees.
Given his positive teaching evaluation, Professor John meets the requirement to propose new courses.
Therefore, Professor John meets all the criteria to propose new courses, according to the premises.
